title: Backtracking
analogy: Backtracking is like navigating a maze — you walk down a path until you hit a dead end, then retrace your steps and try the next branch.

overview.summary: Backtracking systematically explores all candidate solutions by building partial solutions incrementally and abandoning them ('pruning') as soon as they violate constraints.
learningObjectives: Understand the difference between brute-force and backtracking.
overview.interviewRelevance: Backtracking powers classic interview problems like N-Queens, Sudoku, and all permutation/combination generators.

paragraphs: Sudoku solvers, crossword generators, and chess engines all use backtracking to explore moves and undo bad ones.
Your phone's autocorrect tries different letter combinations using backtracking to find valid words.
realWorldHook.ahaMoment: Backtracking is brute-force with a brain — it abandons dead ends early instead of exhausting every possibility.

definition.technicalDefinition: Backtracking is a depth-first search algorithm that builds candidate solutions incrementally and abandons a candidate as soon as it determines the candidate cannot lead to a valid solution.

vocabulary.definition: A tree of all possible states/choices that the algorithm explores.
Cutting off branches of the state space tree that cannot lead to valid solutions.
The condition that stops recursion, either a valid solution or an invalid state.

keyProperties: Explores depth-first and backtracks on constraint violation.
Time complexity is often exponential but pruning significantly reduces actual work.
Space complexity is O(depth) for the recursion stack.

operations.name: Choose-Explore-Unchoose
operations.description: Make a choice, recurse deeper, then undo the choice before trying the next option.
operations.visualNote: The state space tree grows depth-first, and crossed-out branches represent pruned paths.

practice.title: Permutations
practice.keyInsight: Choose each unused element, recurse, then unchoose.

practice.title: Subsets
practice.keyInsight: At each index, include or exclude the element.

practice.title: N-Queens
practice.keyInsight: Place queens row by row, prune columns and diagonals.

practice.title: Word Search
practice.keyInsight: DFS on the grid with visited tracking and backtrack on mismatch.

practice.title: Combination Sum
practice.keyInsight: Allow re-using candidates, prune when sum exceeds target.

practice.title: Sudoku Solver
practice.keyInsight: Try digits 1-9 in each empty cell, backtrack on row/col/box conflict.

conceptQuiz.question: What makes backtracking different from brute-force?
options: Backtracking always runs in polynomial time
conceptQuiz.explanation: Backtracking abandons partial solutions as soon as constraints are violated, avoiding exhaustive enumeration.

conceptQuiz.question: What is the typical time complexity of backtracking?
conceptQuiz.explanation: Backtracking explores a combinatorial state space, which is inherently exponential, though pruning helps in practice.

recognitionQuiz.problemStatement: You need to generate all valid arrangements of N items where each placement must satisfy multiple constraints simultaneously.
recognitionQuiz.explanation: Constraint-satisfaction problems with multiple valid configurations are classic backtracking territory.
